The sytem has two parts: physical device and software on the audio listening device. The physical device has one male jack and one female jack. The male jack is connected to audio listening device. On the female jack you connect your headphones. Open the software and run a ... https://t.co/ziYqBBMqJC

... calibration. The calibration is all done automatically and is as follows: 1) the software sets the volume on the device to zero. 2) volume is increased by one 3) software sends a 0db (max power for that volume level) audio signal to the physical device through the audio ...

... jack. When the device recieves this signal it starts measuring the power sent to the headphones. 4) the software stops sending the signal and starts listening to what is sent throught the audio jack. 5) device sends the measured power to the software, encoded in a signal ...

... transmited through the audio jack. 6) software saves that as a that value. 7) repeat steps 2 to 6 until max volume is reached. Now the software has the max power per volume level given to your headphones by your audio listening device. On the software the user selects what...
...model of headphones being used. From that it’s possible to get the frequency response and sensitivity of the headphones (quite extensive databases available in the internet). ...

Now, based on what sound is being reproduced in the device the software is running, it’s possible to the characteristics of the sound being reproduced by the headphones (amplitude at different frequencies).

@algekalipso Trying to feel the roundness an intuition emerges. My representation of numbers as always been visual, with stairs going bottom left to top right, and the n-th stair having integer multiples (1 to 9) of 10^(n-1).

@algekalipso i.e. first stair has 1, 2, 3, ..., 9 linearly, second stair has 10, 20, 30, ..., 90 linearly, and so on.

The third stair, the thousands stair, feels like the center of mass of the samples that are taken of the number space in my mind, throughout life.
@algekalipso So the thousands stair feels the roundest. Going for number outside integer multiples of 1000 would require a further step of going inside the stair.

@algekalipso Of the possible integer multiples, 8 feels the roundest: very bouba shape and a power of 2 so it is possible to fold the stair into one division.

Thus, 8000

@silverarm0r the basic idea is kind of like exposure therapy, but instead of gradually increasing the exposure to the thing, you increase the similarity of the thing you are exposed to.

@silverarm0r with sound this can be done by having multiple files, each with a changed pitch or speed from the original. you start by listening to the most changed sound, so that it barely sounds like the thing you are afraid of.
@silverarm0r once you are comfortable, you go on to the files that are closer and closer to the actual thing. hopefully it then is comfortable too.

@silverarm0r Changing pitch or speed can continuously change the quality of the experience from something comfortable in the direction of the thing that we want to make comfortable. So the patient can make as little steps as needed, as they feel comfortable.
@silverarm0r And there are so many more ways to change sounds so that the experience can be brought from a different point of comfort into the feared experience.

@silverarm0r Changing the volume and duration is kind of like eating very diluted broccoli soup or very little of it. All you will taste is still the broccoli soup, just staring with very little of that taste.

@silverarm0r Changing pitch and tempo is like eating a mixture of ketchup and broccoli. If you start with 99% ketchup and 1% broccoli, it very much tastes like ketchup. The quality of the experience itself is changed into something not negative, instead of something just a little negative.

Postscope is now a pleasure to use.
One click, and you can visually interact with the discourse in: your timeline, the responses to a post, or a profile.
The data never leaves your computer, and you can save your explorations.
Let me give you a tour of what has already shipped: https://t.co/3iwfs8zAPp

You click this bookmark, and it starts collecting the posts on the page you are seeing replies to a post, your timeline, or a profile.
As it scrolls automatically, you can see how many posts it already got. And at any time, you can jump right into the visualization. https://t.co/FBH5S8k081

Each post is a dot.
The closer two dots are, the more two posts are related.
Posts with more likes are represented by larger dots, so you can see what the egregore values most. https://t.co/pvVWaZWz35

Posts with the same spirit are automatically grouped into clusters that you can name and explore, represented by a color. Read what posts belong to each cluster, who wrote them, and how many likes they got.

If you want, you can see the text right on the map, starting with the most relevant posts, and seeing the rests as you zoom in.
You can even write a mock post and see in real time how it moves around the space of ideas. https://t.co/tqTz1xSOuS

Want to get back to your explorations later? They are saved on your device: your cluster names, sizes, etc.
You can even import / export the data to use on another device or browser, with all the visualizations staying as custom as you made them. https://t.co/dq5YFCXWBi

Want to try this out? Go to https://t.co/8oLnuHfO3d on your computer and drag the button into the bookmark bar. That's it.
Now anytime you are on twitter and want to visually explore the space of ideas that you are seeing, one click is all it takes. https://t.co/AnVqBnMzT8
If anyone wants to mess build upon this, I can make the source code public.

The roadmap includes:
- Creating new visualizations with a focus on global relationships and interpretability, including custom axes
- Improving the clustering quality for the current visualizations
- Auto labeling clusters

Suggestions, requests or any other feedback is welcome
